messages.author: Hugo
messages.text: MeshCore’s SNR-based retransmit delay implicitly prioritizes better-positioned repeaters — nodes with stronger reception retransmit sooner, which can suppress weaker nodes via duplicate detection. However, no packet is ever dropped due to poor signal quality; every repeater in range of a flooded packet will eventually retransmit it (subject to hop count), regardless of link quality. UMSH’s explicit signal-quality thresholds allow the sender to prevent retransmission over weak links entirely, avoiding wasted transmit power on paths unlikely to deliver the packet successfully. Are these not talking about opposite "legs" of a given path? Just because I have weak SNR on the receive side doesn't mean that I don't provide a valuable repeater point from a transmit perspective, right? Eg a situation where, for a given sender, I'm on the edge of where they could successfully transmit a packet. But, I'm well connected in other directions. For instance if the sender is in what amounts to an RF hole/shadow, where I'm the only repeater they have either to connect to the mesh as a whole or in a given direction, but I then repeat their packet on with good connectivity to another part of the mesh that they would otherwise be unable to reach. IOW SNR or minimum RSSI filtering would seem helpful to cut down on noise if I'm a poorly positioned repeater, but could have an adverse effect if I'm a relatively well-positioned repeater that is serving senders that are themselves poorly positioned. Right?
messages.content_html:
MeshCore’s SNR-based retransmit delay implicitly prioritizes better-positioned repeaters — nodes with stronger reception retransmit sooner, which can suppress weaker nodes via duplicate detection. However, no packet is ever dropped due to poor signal quality; every repeater in range of a flooded packet will eventually retransmit it (subject to hop count), regardless of link quality. UMSH’s explicit signal-quality thresholds allow the sender to prevent retransmission over weak links entirely, avoiding wasted transmit power on paths unlikely to deliver the packet successfully.
Are these not talking about opposite "legs" of a given path? Just because I have weak SNR on the receive side doesn't mean that I don't provide a valuable repeater point from a transmit perspective, right? Eg a situation where, for a given sender, I'm on the edge of where they could successfully transmit a packet. But, I'm well connected in other directions. For instance if the sender is in what amounts to an RF hole/shadow, where I'm the only repeater they have either to connect to the mesh as a whole or in a given direction, but I then repeat their packet on with good connectivity to another part of the mesh that they would otherwise be unable to reach. IOW SNR or minimum RSSI filtering would seem helpful to cut down on noise if I'm a poorly positioned repeater, but could have an adverse effect if I'm a relatively well-positioned repeater that is serving senders that are themselves poorly positioned. Right?

messages.author: RQ (darco)
messages.text: What I was trying to figure out a way to work around was a case where a longer path was more reliable, but a shorter, less-reliable path ends up being preferred because a packet or two manages to get through even though the link is fundamentally unreliable. In that case the longer path should be preferred because the packet delivery will be more reliable. I was trying to come up with a mechanism to try and prefer the longer, more reliable path. If a sender establishes a path that is kinda flakey, that sender could then try to find a more reliable path by trying to trace out a new path with a minimum SNR option. So the logic would be, find a path that works, but if that path seems to be flakey then try to find a more reliable path with a better SNR using those options. Repeaters would only set minimum SNR configurations in really dense deployments. At least, that's the idea. (edited)
messages.content_html: What I was trying to figure out a way to work around was a case where a longer path was more reliable, but a shorter, less-reliable path ends up being preferred because a packet or two manages to get through even though the link is fundamentally unreliable. In that case the longer path should be preferred because the packet delivery will be more reliable. I was trying to come up with a mechanism to try and prefer the longer, more reliable path. If a sender establishes a path that is kinda flakey, that sender could then try to find a more reliable path by trying to trace out a new path with a minimum SNR option. So the logic would be, find a path that works, but if that path seems to be flakey then try to find a more reliable path with a better SNR using those options. Repeaters would only set minimum SNR configurations in really dense deployments. At least, that's the idea. (edited)

messages.author: Hugo
messages.text: "Longer" meaning number of hops, yea? So, in this situation, the repeaters themselves may not have minimum SNR set, but the ability for the client allows it to, basically, proactively probe for other viable paths that do have a better SNR floor, such that it can potentially replace its initial discovered path that would otherwise be more preferred given standard path discovery? Whereas for the repeater side configuration option, that would more be something like a noise mitigating control, that in a dense mesh of repeaters provides more "crowded" repeaters to basically say "I know that there are a bunch of other repeaters around, so rather than repeating very faint messages, I'm going to assume others will take care of that and I'll not add to the noise". Something like that?

Whereas for the repeater side configuration option, that would more be something like a noise mitigating control, that in a dense mesh of repeaters provides more "crowded" repeaters to basically say "I know that there are a bunch of other repeaters around, so rather than repeating very faint messages, I'm going to assume others will take care of that and I'll not add to the noise". Something like that?
